elements.sent-2411-001: Answering end user security questions is challenging.
elements.sent-2411-002: While large language models (LLMs) like GPT, LLAMA, and Gemini are far from error-free, they have shown promise in answering a variety of questions outside of security.
elements.sent-2411-003: We studied LLM performance in the area of end user security by qualitatively evaluating 3 popular LLMs on 900 systematically collected end user security questions.
elements.sent-2411-004: While LLMs demonstrate broad generalist ``knowledge'' of end user security information, there are patterns of errors and limitations across LLMs consisting of stale and inaccurate answers, and indirect or unresponsive communication styles, all of which impacts the quality of information received.
elements.sent-2411-005: Based on these patterns, we suggest directions for model improvement and recommend user strategies for interacting with LLMs when seeking assistance with security.
elements.sent-2411-006: Large language model-based conversational assistants (``LLMs'' hereinafter) have grown increasingly popular among users for distilling complex information in user-friendly ways.
elements.sent-2411-007: This capability has made LLMs an enticing resource for computer security and online safety.
elements.sent-2411-008: For instance, they provide financial advice as chatbots \cite{next_web , and are endorsed as reliable tools for fraud detection by security vendors \cite{zdnet_norton, biocatch, dataleon .}}
elements.sent-2411-009: The use of LLMs for computer security advice is the natural evolution of the user practice of seeking online advice for security-relevant tasks such as strong password creation \cite{redmiles-2016-sources-n-selection .}
elements.sent-2411-010: However, the quality of LLM responses to end user security questions has not been rigorously studied.
elements.sent-2411-011: There is a critical need to evaluate the quality of LLM support in the user security context as adoption accelerates.
elements.sent-2411-012: There are already well-documented risks to relying on LLMs for information broadly, including hallucinations, challenges in reasoning, and vulnerability to concept drift~\cite{openai2024gpt4, GPT4SystemCard, geminiteam2024geminiv1, brown2020language, rajani-etal-2019-explain, shwartz-etal-2020-unsupervised, Lu_2018, bommasani2022opportunitiesrisksfoundationmodels .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Further, LLMs have been found to perpetuate known misconceptions in security and online safety~\cite{chen2023can .}
elements.sent-2411-014: A larger and broader evaluation of LLM security responses (beyond known misconceptions \cite{chen2023can ) is needed in order to comprehensively outline the boundaries of LLM security ``knowledge” to inform users about LLM shortcomings, and support future model development and improvement.}
elements.sent-2411-015: To address this gap, we evaluate the quality of responses generated by three LLMs---GPT, LLaMA, and Gemini---to end user questions about security and online safety.
elements.sent-2411-016: We first collected user security and online safety questions covering 7 security areas (many of which are aligned with prior work \cite{redmiles2020webadvice ) from frequently asked questions (FAQs) published by various consumer safety and commercial organizations (Section \ref{non-exp-question-collection ).}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Then, for each question, we identified authoritative sources for assessing the quality of the generated LLM responses.
elements.sent-2411-018: Finally, we conducted a qualitative assessment following the information integrity framework \cite{flowerday-2007 , of the $1,244$ LLM responses (generated using ``zero-shot prompting'' \cite{kojima2023large ) to measure their quality, specifically, their accuracy, completeness, and relevance.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: In addition, we evaluated an aspect of communication style, directness, in which necessary and important information is presented first to make efficient use of user attention \cite{ Nielsen1997 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Our assessment is thus guided by the following research questions (RQs):.
elements.sent-2411-021: What is the information quality of LLM responses to user security questions?.
elements.sent-2411-022: What are the patterns of deficient or erroneous LLM responses to user security questions?.
elements.sent-2411-023: What actionable suggestions can improve LLM performance for users seeking security advice and developers building models?.
elements.sent-2411-024: We found that LLMs provide high quality information expressed in a user-friendly manner when prompted with general knowledge security questions.
elements.sent-2411-025: However, we also found consistent deficiencies and errors that are present across LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-026: Surfacing these limitations can help users more effectively engage with LLMs and support model development improvements.
elements.sent-2411-027: In particular, LLMs often fail to leverage research findings that could improve communication of important nuances in guidance and reduce misinterpretation of more specialized concepts.
elements.sent-2411-028: For example, LLMs provide outdated password guidance, neglect the risks of app permissions, fail to connect phishing and HTTPS URLs, and confuse zero-knowledge proofs and zero-knowledge encryption---all topics well-studied in research.
elements.sent-2411-029: In addition, our findings tentatively suggest LLMs may be influenced by marketing materials in that they tend to over-promise the capabilities of technology and products (e.g., endorsing VPNs for phishing protection), and miss important criticisms (e.g., neglecting the transparency benefits of open source code and data breach handling).
elements.sent-2411-030: We also note several instances in which safety guardrails appear to block users from receiving answers to benign security questions.
elements.sent-2411-031: Finally, we find patterns of indirectness in communication style across LLMs, for example, initiating responses with encouraging, generic statements (e.g., ``Great question!’’), or other information that has not been asked for and may already be known by the user (e.g., definitions).
elements.sent-2411-032: We term the latter ``LLM-splaining’’ given its similarity to mansplaining \cite{solnit2014men .}
elements.sent-2411-033: We make the following contributions to understanding LLM ability to answer computer security questions for end users:.
elements.sent-2411-034: A diverse set of LLM ``knowledge’’ deficiencies in computer security: Through inductive thematic analysis on 1244 responses across the 3 LLMs, we found multiple deficiency patterns, some of which can lead to complete user-LLM interaction failures in which LLM responses are not relevant to the user question.
elements.sent-2411-035: These patterns inform guidance for users and model developers (\Cref{findings,sec:discussion ).}
elements.sent-2411-036: A corpus of user security questions with authoritative answer sources: We curated and will publish a collection of user-focused security questions from 7 categories with sources of authoritative answers for use by future research and development projects (\Cref{sec:method ).}
elements.sent-2411-037: A framework for assessing LLMs: We adapted information integrity concepts to form a framework for the qualitative assessment of the content and communication style of LLM responses (\Cref{sec:method ).}

elements.sent-2411-056: We explored the RQs through three tasks.
elements.sent-2411-057: First, we built a corpus of questions and responses reflecting end user security concerns.
elements.sent-2411-058: Then, we formulated prompts based on the questions to evaluate multiple LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-059: Finally, we assessed the information quality and directness of the LLM responses.
elements.sent-2411-060: In this section we describe how we implemented these tasks to prioritize common end-user questions and reliably measure LLM performance on the questions.
elements.sent-2411-061: Answering our RQs requires a corpus of questions representative of common end user security concerns.
elements.sent-2411-062: We were unable to obtain any corpus from prior work.
elements.sent-2411-063: Chen et al. had studied common user security misconceptions, but the questions used were generated to follow a limited template (e.g. ``I’ve heard of this claim: \{MISCONCEPTION\}.
elements.sent-2411-065: Redmiles et al. had gathered a corpus of $140$ user security questions across 12 security and privacy topics to assess the quality of web security / privacy advice, but the corpus was not available in its entirety.\footnote{Learned through communication with the authors.}
elements.sent-2411-066: We found other potential user security-oriented datasets that were not worded as questions or were tailored to particular platforms (e.g., \cite{pattnaik2023perspectives ) and geographic regions (e.g., \cite{hasegawa-2022-non-experts-snp-qna ).}}
elements.sent-2411-067: Given the gap in question sources, we built a corpus of questions by mining dominant security topics and using them alongisde question templates to extract user questions from {trusted resources .}
elements.sent-2411-068: We define trusted resources as organizations that either have a mission tied to user security (e.g. government agencies, and universities) or a business that strongly depends on customer security (e.g., financial services, and security vendors).\footnote{Our trusted resources collection includes security vendors, government agencies, non-profit organizations, financial institutions, university cybersecurity guides, and product websites (e.g., social media, browsers, and email providers).}
elements.sent-2411-069: Some examples of trusted resources are the NIST, NCSC, CISA, NCA, Canadian Centre for Cyber Security (CCCS), Federal Communications Commission (FCC), Federal Trade Commission (FTC) and the Electronic Frontier Foundation (EFF).. Dominant security topics.
elements.sent-2411-070: Through a thematic analysis of security content trusted resources made available to users, namely, National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) \cite{NIST , National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) \cite{NCSC , Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) \cite{CISA , and National Cybersecurity Alliance (NCA) \cite{NCA , we identified seven high level security categories and underlying fine grained security topics.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: Our Authentication, Software Updates and Safe Browsing categories appear to align well with categories in \cite{redmiles2020webadvice (e.g. ``Passwords’’ and ``Network Security’’, ``Software’’, ``Browsers’’, respectively) although the questions in our respective corpora likely differ.}
elements.sent-2411-073: To collect questions in the 7 security categories we developed Google search queries related to topics, some examples of which are in \Cref{tab:categories-table .}
elements.sent-2411-074: With the queries we identified websites meeting our trusted resource criteria that are popular sources of security guidance and mined them for FAQs guides.
elements.sent-2411-075: We included product sites (Chrome, Edge, Facebook, Gmail, etc.) to collect security FAQs that are relevant to users.
elements.sent-2411-076: Questions from these included “How to secure a hacked or compromised Google Account?”, “How to recover Instagram account for which I lost access to linked phone number?”, etc. We also used templates to construct questions related to the 7 categories.
elements.sent-2411-077: In particular, we used 2 templates: ``What is [insert security concept, technology or product]’’ and ``Why should [insert a security related behavior, practice or attitude]’’.
elements.sent-2411-078: For example, “Why should I trust [password manager] with my passwords?", where [password manager] is replaced with popular password managers; or ``What is a [type of scam]?", where [type of scam] is replaced with various types (e.g. romance scam, lottery scam, pig butchering scam).
elements.sent-2411-079: In the end, we had collected questions from more than $50$ trusted resources in our corpus.
elements.sent-2411-080: For each question, we also identified authoritative sources with which to verify LLM responses later in our study.
elements.sent-2411-081: All authoritative sources were published by organizations meeting our trusted resource criteria.
elements.sent-2411-082: Often the website containing an FAQ was found to be an authoritative source for that question.
elements.sent-2411-083: However, we also drew upon the trusted resources to serve as authoritative sources for questions in many categories.
elements.sent-2411-084: In case of multiple authoritative sources for a question, we used all authoritative sources and distilled information using our subject matter expertise to assess responses.
elements.sent-2411-085: All of our authoritative sources were reviewed and confirmed to be reliable by cybersecurity subject matter experts at a large financial services institution.
elements.sent-2411-086: To classify the questions by the type of security information sought, two researchers reached agreement that each question is most aligned with one of the following areas: glossary and facts, guidance and best practices, attacks, trends, and risk assessment.
elements.sent-2411-087: In addition, we also classified questions by knowledge category, factual, conceptual, and procedural, as in Bloom's taxonomy \cite{blooms-taxonomy-2001 .}
elements.sent-2411-088: We will make the questions and their categorizations publicly available.

elements.sent-2411-092: We carefully reviewed LLM responses, assessing both content and communication style.
elements.sent-2411-093: With respect to content, we focus on information integrity and evaluate the accuracy, completeness, and relevancy of LLM responses as in Flowerday et al. \cite{flowerday-2007 .}
elements.sent-2411-094: We also consider communication style by way of directness (also known as the inverted pyramid style of writing \cite{zinsser2001writing ), where necessary and important information is presented first.}
elements.sent-2411-095: This communication style is often recommended for web \cite{Schade2018, Nielsen1996, Nielsen1997 and nonfiction writing.}
elements.sent-2411-096: For our study, we define evaluation criteria as the following:.
elements.sent-2411-097: Accuracy: The proportion of verifiably correct information.
elements.sent-2411-098: Thoroughness: The degree to which all necessary answer information is provided.
elements.sent-2411-099: Relevancy: The proportion of information that is relevant.
elements.sent-2411-100: Directness: Whether the initial text is responsive to the question asked.
elements.sent-2411-101: Since there was no established codebook for interpreting these attributes in the user security context; we developed a codebook (to be made available) that enabled the authors to consistently assess these attributes on a 3-point Likert scale.
elements.sent-2411-102: Each author coded LLM responses manually to evaluate the 4 attributes.
elements.sent-2411-103: The authors used information present in the authoritative source of the question, along with their subject matter expertise, to assess all the facts in a response by following the detailed definition of four earlier mentioned criteria to best of their ability.
elements.sent-2411-104: In our codebook, the 3-point Likert scale for accuracy, thoroughness and relevancy is summarized as: the attribute is completely achieved (e.g., in the case of accuracy, all information is determined to be correct), the attribute is partially achieved, and the attribute is only minimally achieved.
elements.sent-2411-105: Directness is assessed as a binary label, i.e., the initial LLM text is directly responsive to the question asked or it is not.
elements.sent-2411-106: The authors also added notes explaining their evaluation of the attributes; these notes facilitated the analysis in Section~\ref{overall-evaluation .}
elements.sent-2411-107: Examples of coded responses and the complete definition of each code point and its options in our codebook can be accessed \href{https://docs.google.com/document/d/e/2PACX-1vQ99tup4hftyJMDTYlRgL41AM560v-fFqWfSQzb2-G1emV-j_etghUB0-DYL0j9cMSQX7AYjF9d5Ojt/pub {here .}}
elements.sent-2411-108: These attributes are motivated by the need to manage risk in AI systems and past research.
elements.sent-2411-109: For example, a correct but incomplete answer could create user risk.
elements.sent-2411-110: Relevancy is important given user difficulty with prioritizing advice \cite{redmiles2020webadvice and reconciling contradictions \cite{murray-2023-costbenefitsofadvice .}}
elements.sent-2411-111: Finally, directness reduces the risk of losing user attention due to unnecessary information \cite{Nielsen1997 .}
elements.sent-2411-112: To measure the quality of responses we followed the standard practice of qualitative coding.
elements.sent-2411-113: First, we sampled a small percentage of questions and coded their responses over multiple rounds.
elements.sent-2411-114: We used Fleiss Kappa for IRR calculation because we had more than two coders \cite{mchugh2012-irr .}
elements.sent-2411-115: In our study, 4 coders coded 72 GPT responses independently across 3 coding rounds, and achieved IRR with substantial agreement on each code point \cite{landis1977-irr-numbers .}
elements.sent-2411-116: Our Fleiss Kappa for the third coding round was 0.73, 0.63, and 0.76 for Accuracy, Thoroughness, and Directness, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-117: We could not calculate Kappa for Relevancy because a single rating (“Relevant" with 100\% agreement) was assigned to all the questions we coded.
elements.sent-2411-118: After IRR establishment, we divided the rest of the question responses among the coders for evaluation.

elements.sent-2411-130: Before making conclusions about LLM performance, it is necessary to gauge response reliability over multiple invocations of the same, or semantically similar, prompts.

elements.sent-2411-132: To the best of our knowledge, LLM response reliability has not been studied for open-ended user security questions, so we conducted two analyses to gauge response reliability for end user security questions.
elements.sent-2411-133: Our methodology to measure reliability in LLM responses across paraphrased questions (referred to as ``queries'' in \cite{chen2023can ) was the following: \begin{enumerate [leftmargin=*] \item.}}

elements.sent-2411-136: Using these Google APIs 16 questions were paraphrased 274 times, referred as paraphrased questions (PQs) here; then using the transformer-based model Universal Semantic Encoder (USE) \cite{tensorflow-USE we measured how semantically close a Google API paraphrase is to the OQ to reduce the list of PQs to 157 for 15 OQs.}
elements.sent-2411-137: Finally, 4 researches manually verified whether the remaining 157 PQs for the 15 OQs were good paraphrasings.

elements.sent-2411-329: Our evaluation found a high rate of errors (31\% for GPT) and omissions of important information (43\% for GPT) in response to procedural questions.

elements.sent-2411-331: For example, when asked ``What age groups most report fraud or scams?'', GPT calls out 4 age groups, including teens, adults and seniors, when in fact seniors are less likely to report fraud and scams than younger people \cite{ftc_scams_all_ages_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-332: Another challenging area is questions that are sensitive to recent events, such as queries regarding trust and reputation.
elements.sent-2411-333: For example, when asked ``Can I get a free password manager?'', GPT suggests Lastpass (among others), a password manager that is no longer recommended due to recent breaches.
elements.sent-2411-334: Finally, we encourage care around questions in the areas of authentication and antiviruses, particularly when asking product, platform or company-related questions, since significant weakness in response quality was found in both.
elements.sent-2411-335: GPT errors exceeded 30\% in both categories, and the same weakness was present in both Llama and Gemini responses.
elements.sent-2411-336: Areas of strength for LLMs are definitions of security concepts (e.g., ``What is malware?''), basic risk assessment questions (e.g., ``Will 2 factor authentication protect me?'') and core functionality of established products (e.g., ``How does Keeper password manager work?'').
elements.sent-2411-337: This is compatible with the fact that factual and conceptual questions are the most likely to be answered correctly (\Cref{tab:gpt-evaluation-dist-by-topics ).}
elements.sent-2411-338: We used questions from published FAQs and question templates that our own review found to be written in ``plain’’ English (i.e., no technical, rare or otherwise lesser known words and phrases \cite{williams2004legal ).}
elements.sent-2411-339: We conducted a small experiment to test sensitivity of GPT to rephrased questions from this corpus (Section~\ref{sec:reproducibility ) and did not find sensitivity to phrasing.}
elements.sent-2411-340: However, we note that the question of whether LLMs are sensitive to phrasing in organic user security questions has not been addressed by our experiments and some sensitivity to phrasing was found in \cite{chen2023can .}
elements.sent-2411-341: In addition, there are several other open prompt engineering questions that would allow for important extensions of user guidance in this area.
elements.sent-2411-342: For example, we found a substantial number of indirect responses (17\% with GPT) that delay information delivery and could cause user attention to shift elsewhere.
elements.sent-2411-343: We term a portion of these responses ``LLM-splaining’’ because they provide information that is likely already known by the user.
elements.sent-2411-344: The unnecessary repetition of information may diminish user trust \cite{zeffane2011communication .}
elements.sent-2411-345: For example, to the question, ``How do I set up Okta Verify?’’, GPT responds with 2 sentences explaining Okta Verify before beginning to answer the user question: ``Okta Verify is an authentication app that works in conjunction with Okta, a secure identity management service.
elements.sent-2411-347: Prompts that encourage LLMs to consistently address user questions directly are an open problem.
elements.sent-2411-348: Finally, we note that while our questions did not contain any sensitive information, security questions can involve sensitive data and so it is quite possible user prompts will be sensitive, particularly in light of patterns of over-sharing with LLMs that have been found \cite{zhang2024s .}
elements.sent-2411-349: The relationship between LLM response quality and the inclusion of sensitive information is largely unexplored and would be a useful addition to user guidance.
elements.sent-2411-350: While this may change with LLM-enabled search, none of the LLMs we experimented with routinely cite sources when answering end user security questions.
elements.sent-2411-351: Indeed, URLs are present in less than 5\% of the GPT answers in our evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-352: We recommend users explicitly ask for authoritative sources supporting LLM responses, and consider requesting that LLMs find supporting data from government or nonprofits sources of the user’s choosing (e.g., NIST, FTC).
elements.sent-2411-353: As discussed in \cite{barman2024beyond , ``simple heuristics to detect potential errors or misinformation from LLMs’’ should be part of a user toolkit for safe interaction with LLMs.}
elements.sent-2411-354: Towards such a toolkit we see evidence of two heuristics in our data given that errors in some of the evaluation criteria are predictive of other errors.
elements.sent-2411-355: In particular, indirect GPT responses ($n=153$) have errors in accuracy, thoroughness and relevance with probability greater than $.58$.
elements.sent-2411-356: Similarly, irrelevant GPT answers ($n=15$) have errors in accuracy, thoroughness or directness with probably greater than $.73$.
elements.sent-2411-357: The support for each of these heuristics is relatively low, so it is an open problem to confirm that these heuristics hold.
elements.sent-2411-358: That said, they suggest a promising direction for helping users safely interact with LLMs since indirect responses and irrelevant content may be more easily recognized than the other criteria and serve as an early user warning that a response may be low quality.
elements.sent-2411-359: While there has been recent progress in computational approaches to predicting hallucinations \cite{farquhar2024detecting , user friendly heuristics are an important open problem.}
elements.sent-2411-360: In this section we discuss the primary areas of performance problems in our evaluation, organized according to the user challenges described at the beginning of Section~\ref{sec:discussion .}
elements.sent-2411-361: While we suggest directions for improvements, we are not prescriptive, as model developers are better positioned to identify root causes and craft solutions.
elements.sent-2411-362: Our evaluation suggests augmenting LLM training data with more security research and reducing the support of security product marketing materials will improve LLM performance.
elements.sent-2411-363: For example, all LLMs in our evaluation routinely provide stale password guidance despite ample research attesting to the usability shortcomings of such guidance.
elements.sent-2411-364: In addition, the privacy and security risks of default app permissions, a topic well-covered by research \cite{felt-permissions-2011, chia-permission-risks-2012, calciati-auto-permissions-2020 , are often overlooked in LLM responses.}
elements.sent-2411-365: Finally, the incorrectly inflated capabilities of certain security technologies (e.g., HTTPS) suggest marketing materials may have high support in LLM training data.
elements.sent-2411-366: For example, “Authy takes security very seriously", and “Dashlane is a well-established and reputable password manager that has earned the trust of millions of users worldwide." Overly favorable product content may be perceived as marketing information and can be de-valued by users \cite{redmiles2016learned .}
elements.sent-2411-367: Questions involving specific companies, products or platforms are often challenging for LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-368: For example, when no company, product or platform is mentioned, more than 60\% of GPT responses are perfectly accurate, thorough and relevant.
elements.sent-2411-369: In contrast, this number drops to less than 44\% when a specific company, product or platform is part of the prompt.
elements.sent-2411-370: We suggest the presence of a company name, product or platform may be useful for developers in determining when to prioritize authoritative sources.
elements.sent-2411-371: Finally, while few in number, our evaluation did surface 10 examples in which the human-LLM interaction completely failed because the LLM was not responsive to the question.
elements.sent-2411-372: In 7 of these (6 for Gemini and 1 for LLaMA), this appeared to be due to mistriggered guardrails; the 3 others were GPT misinterpretations (e.g., to the question, ``Can you write your login credentials down, really?’’ GPT responds, ``I'm sorry for any confusion, but as an AI, I don't possess personal login credentials…”).
elements.sent-2411-373: We suggest that developers consider how to better confirm the user intent before delivering a non-answer.
elements.sent-2411-374: In short, while those with a complete view of the model development process are best positioned determine how to address these errors as well as the hallucinations and omitted risks discussed in Section~\ref{findings , we emphasize that answers to questions in these areas are well documented in authoritative security sources (e.g., peer-reviewed research papers, government reports, product and platform documentation, etc.) and so it appears models either do not have sufficient access to this data or aren’t prioritizing data sources appropriately.}
elements.paragraph-2411-039: Aligning mental models with sound security practices.
elements.sent-2411-375: Our question corpus was gathered with the goal of broadly representing user concerns in security, rather than focusing on areas known to have potentially problematic user models.
elements.sent-2411-376: That said, we do see evidence of known user mental models in LLM answers.
elements.sent-2411-377: For example, \cite{wash2010folk found that the term ``hacker'' is often used to broadly describe bad actors in a security context regardless of the actor's attack strategy or relationship to the user.}
elements.sent-2411-378: We see a similar broad use of the term in LLM responses, in particular, GPT characterizes actors launching technical attacks as hackers (``The longer the password, the more combinations a hacker has to try to crack it.") as well as scammers (``Hackers can use sophisticated phishing schemes to trick users").
elements.sent-2411-379: In addition, the inflation of the protective power of HTTPS noted in Section~\ref{findings was also found in \cite{herbert2023world among participants in India.}}
elements.sent-2411-380: We emphasize that LLM perpetuation of user mental models is only problematic if those models are associated with risky security practices.
elements.sent-2411-381: For example, ``hacker'' terminology may discourage awareness of the fact that security risks also exist with people a user knows (e.g., as in intimate partner or elder abuse).
elements.sent-2411-382: Finally, we note that while some LLM error patterns may not specifically perpetuate problematic mental models, they may still contribute to areas of confusion.
elements.sent-2411-383: In particular, while we are not aware of previous research around the mental models of linking VPNs to phishing protection, the protective power of VPNs is a well-established area of user confusion (e.g., \cite{dutkowska2022and, binkhorst2022security ).}
elements.sent-2411-384: We encourage model developers to use security user mental models research to explore potential areas of model bias and consider relying more heavily on authoritative sources in those areas.
elements.sent-2411-385: We recommend developers tailor the balance between content and sources based on the nature of the user question.
elements.sent-2411-386: In particular, consider deferring to sources in the cases of procedural and UI-dependent questions, or when contact information or URLs are part of the response.
elements.sent-2411-387: For instance, GPT provided email addresses in 6 responses, none of which were valid at the time of our evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-388: Also, in 3 responses, GPT provided 5 (out of 123 URLs across 47 responses) inaccessible URLs.
elements.sent-2411-389: Our evaluation indicates making strategic choices between the balance of content and sources in LLM answers will improve response quality and may increase user acceptance; there is evidence that acceptance can be sensitive to the authoritativeness of the source, for example in areas that are less familiar to the user \cite{redmiles2016learned .}
elements.sent-2411-390: Given that user security questions may be time-sensitive, we recommend LLMs use a direct communication style in response to such questions.
elements.sent-2411-391: In addition, developers should consider signaling to the user when they are not able to deliver a high-confidence response, similar to the warnings many LLMs already provide about potential limitations due to the date range of training data.
elements.sent-2411-392: Finally, we raise the question of whether explanations can also help the end user better detect errors in the end user security context \cite{bansal2021does .}
elements.sent-2411-393: The findings of this study depend on the questions used for the assessment.
elements.sent-2411-394: As for the computer security questions, there is no definite and exhaustive list, so we acknowledge limiting our assessment to the best of our ability to curate end-user security questions.
elements.sent-2411-395: Since we have not recruited users in our study to ask security questions, we acknowledge that in our questions we might miss the exact natural language users use to interact with LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-396: The natural language of user interaction with systems evolves and changes with time --- known from the evolution of Google search queries \cite{evolution-of-search --- so our study would be relevant over time by not including the questions in users' voices.}
elements.sent-2411-397: Users' natural language of interaction with generative AI, and exhaustive assessment of security-related questions are left as future work.
elements.sent-2411-398: We also acknowledge that our work is restricted to security-related end-user questions, due to the limited human evaluation capability and time availability.
elements.sent-2411-399: Another important aspect of digital security is privacy, the assessment of LLMs in the privacy domain is left as future work.
elements.sent-2411-400: Lastly, the error reasons we hypothesized are our speculations based on issues mentioned in NLP literature about LLMs, but we haven't verified them.
elements.sent-2411-401: Especially training data-related hypotheses are challenging to verify because none of the model developers released the data sources.
elements.sent-2411-402: Also, our recommendations for developers are based on prior works but are not verified if they will successfully solve the issues we have found.
elements.sent-2411-403: For example, aligning models to ask for user clarification, how to fine-tune LLMs that would prioritize research findings over marketing materials, etc. Our usability recommendations are not tested on actual users to measure their efficacy.
elements.sent-2411-404: We leave all this to future work.
elements.sent-2411-405: Researchers should also consider the evaluation of AI-powered search engines that generate responses with sources (e.g., ChatGPT search, Perplexity AI search).
elements.sent-2411-406: In our study we have focused on the evaluation of response content, not on what sources and how they are used to generate responses.
elements.sent-2411-407: Besides that, at the time of our study, these search engines were being introduced and APIs for them were not available, making them difficult to evaluate at our scale.
elements.sent-2411-408: Future work could look into what sources are suggested, and how they are used in AI-powered search engines to produce responses.
elements.sent-2411-409: Our study evaluates security information provided by LLMs on 7 security topics.
elements.sent-2411-410: To support future research we will make the question corpus and our codebooks available.
elements.sent-2411-411: Our study has been reviewed from an ethical standpoint and determined to be out of scope of the authors' university IRB approval process.
elements.sent-2411-412: Our study is approved by the legal and ethical review process of the industry author's organization.
elements.sent-2411-413: While we have evaluated LLM responses to questions that reflect user concerns, none of the questions include personal user information and, to the best of our knowledge, none are verbatim user questions and so are unlikely to include identifying language.
elements.sent-2411-414: In addition, we used paid subscriptions to the GPT and Gemini APIs which ensures user data (prompts) and responses are not used for future model development, as per OpenAI and Google privacy policies at the time of our experiments.
elements.sent-2411-415: Finally, we note that while we surfaced error patterns in LLM responses that negatively impact the user experience, we do not believe any present an urgent risk, hence we are not planning to disclose our findings to model development companies prior to publication.. .

elements.sent-2411-431: Below, we list the additional failure patterns we observed during our analysis of all the LLMs but deemed less important than the ones mentioned in the \Cref{findings .}
elements.sent-2411-432: Q: “What kinds of devices can support antivirus/antimalware software?".
elements.sent-2411-433: Gaming Consoles: Some gaming consoles like PlayStation and Xbox have built-in antivirus capabilities or allow installation of third-party security software.
elements.sent-2411-434: Internet of Things (IoT) Devices: Many IoT devices, such as smart home devices, industrial control systems, and medical devices, can run lightweight antivirus software to protect against malware.
elements.sent-2411-435: NAS (Network-Attached Storage) Devices: Some NAS devices, which are used for storing and sharing files on a network, can run antivirus software to scan files and prevent malware infections.
elements.sent-2411-436: Q: “Do I need a separate security key for every computer I use?".
elements.sent-2411-437: Device-specific pairing: Security keys, like YubiKeys or Google Titan Security Keys, often pair with a specific device and browser combination.
elements.sent-2411-438: This pairing ensures that the key only works on that particular device and browser.
elements.sent-2411-439: Having a separate key for each computer you use helps maintain this secure pairing.
elements.sent-2411-441: Passwords are typically encrypted using a process called “hashing".
elements.sent-2411-442: Q: “What are some of the security risks associated with Bluetooth?".
elements.sent-2411-443: Use a VPN when connecting to public Wi-Fi: This can help protect your data from being intercepted.
elements.sent-2411-444: Passwords are encrypted using a variety of methods, but the most common is **hashing**.
elements.sent-2411-445: Q: “What if I accidentally leave my phone at home or my phone is out of charge or broken, how would I use Okta to authenticate?".
elements.sent-2411-446: Password Managers: If you use a password manager (like LastPass or 1Password) that stores your Okta credentials, you can try using it to authenticate.
elements.sent-2411-447: All the LLMs in our evaluation omitted and listed incorrect mobile permissions used by different security products, like AV, password manager, and MFA applications, and sometimes failed to explain why permission was used on mobiles or computers.
elements.sent-2411-448: They often mentioned AVs needed camera, microphone, phone, and SMS permissions, but these are not listed in their official documentation.
elements.sent-2411-449: When explaining why permissions like location and nativeMessaging were used by MFA mobile applications and browser extensions their responses failed to mention the reason is access control and to support biometrics.
elements.sent-2411-450: All the LLMs were not completely thorough in explaining scams in terms of what exactly a scam is about, who are the target audience and who report more, recent trends, and their consequences.
elements.sent-2411-451: When explaining cryptocurrency scams they didn't mention these scams ask anyone to invest money and do not just target people with cryptocurrencies.
elements.sent-2411-452: GPT failed to explain the AV software scam.
elements.sent-2411-453: When asked about the target audience and report they conflated targeting and reporting and didn't mention that the elderly population (65+) gets targeted more and young adults (18 - 65) report more scams \cite{ftc_scams_all_ages_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-454: LLaMA and GPT didn't mention that the underage (\textless18) population also experiences and reports scams \cite{ftc_consumer_sentinal_scam_by_age_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-455: Any models didn't mention that scams could lead to computation resource misuse, not just financial loss.
elements.sent-2411-456: We also observed that LLMs often made up facts when asked about the capabilities of AVs in general.
elements.sent-2411-457: They suggested AVs are available for IoT and network-attached storage (NAS) devices, and gaming consoles.
elements.sent-2411-458: GPT made up a new term “File emulation" to describe how antivirus works.
elements.sent-2411-459: GPT and LLaMA often made unverifiable claims that AVs are “designed to be lightweight and efficient" and “optimized" for best performance and don't cause any battery issues.
elements.sent-2411-460: In contrast, users have reported these concerns on AV forums and Reddit communities.
elements.sent-2411-461: LLaMA made up numbers about the percentage of battery consumption per hour by different AVs and compared it with tasks like browsing Facebook, watching YouTube videos, and playing the Candy Crush game.
elements.sent-2411-462: They often exaggerated the capabilities of features offered by different AVs in our corpus which are not mentioned on official AV websites.
elements.sent-2411-463: Examples of these include mentioning features like call blockers, and anti-theft that don't exist.
elements.sent-2411-464: For ransomware-specific features, all LLMs claimed AVs detect suspicious behavior in real-time to prevent encryption of important files, contrary to the permission-based block list implemented by AVs.

elements.subsection-2411-013: Error Pattern Discovery in LLM Responses
elements.paragraph-2411-061: Qualitative Analysis of Imperfect GPT Responses.
elements.sent-2411-641: In our qualitative analysis of imperfect GPT responses, we first conducted a manual review to identify recurring errors—specifically, mistakes or knowledge gaps present across multiple outputs.
elements.sent-2411-642: To catalog these errors systematically, we employed an inductive thematic analysis \cite{braun-2006-thematic-analysis .}
elements.sent-2411-643: We revisited every response that failed to satisfy at least one of our four evaluation criteria—accuracy, thoroughness, relevance, and directness—and assigned each a concise error label based on the coder’s notes.
elements.sent-2411-644: We then examined these labels and merged any that described similar error patterns, discarding labels that did not recur.
elements.sent-2411-645: Next, to determine whether the error patterns we identified in GPT generalized to other models, we applied the same coding scheme to responses generated by Llama and Gemini.
elements.sent-2411-646: Given the considerable time required for manual evaluation, we limited our assessment to the subset of \geminiQuestionsEvaluated questions for which GPT had produced imperfect responses.

elements.sent-2411-652: In our evaluation, GPT, Llama, and Gemini attempted to answer almost all the questions in our corpus, however, often with errors in correctness, thoroughness, or relevancy.
elements.sent-2411-653: In this section, we briefly discuss the evaluation results, then \add{generalizable error patterns seen across the three models in more detail, along with cause hypotheses.}
elements.sent-2411-654: Over 6 months, we evaluated 900 GPT responses (including establishing a high IRR).
elements.sent-2411-655: We do not report quantitative evaluation results for Llama and Gemini, as their responses were only assessed for a subset of questions where GPT had previously failed, see \Cref{overall-evaluation .}
elements.sent-2411-656: Reporting these selective results could lead to misleading comparisons of overall model performance.
elements.sent-2411-657: First, there is a downward trend in GPT's accuracy from factual (highest accuracy), conceptual, to procedural questions (lowest accuracy), where knowledge category is listed in order of cognitive processing required \cite{blooms-taxonomy-2001 .}
elements.sent-2411-658: We hypothesize LLMs may not be good at synthesizing multiple pieces of information and formulating new facts.
elements.sent-2411-659: Second, GPT's overall performance—and its performance within individual security categories—is lower on questions that involve specific products, platforms, or companies (398 out of 900 questions).
elements.sent-2411-660: When a question is related to a product, platform, or company, the probability of the GPT response being fully accurate, thorough, and relevant is 44\%.
elements.sent-2411-661: This probability increases to 60\% for questions that are not associated with such entities.
elements.sent-2411-662: Lastly, GPT performs particularly poorly in the authentication and antivirus/anti-malware security categories.
elements.sent-2411-663: This underperformance may be attributed to the high proportion (81.15\%) of product-, platform-, or company-specific questions within these categories.
elements.sent-2411-664: Following this evaluation, we present the most common error and deficiency patterns identified across all three LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-665: Through our manual expert evaluation and inductive thematic analysis, we identified several recurring error patterns across responses from multiple LLMs that have the potential to negatively impact user security.
elements.sent-2411-666: We organized these errors into two high-level categories: content-related errors, which involve incorrect or deficient information, and communication-related errors, which pertain to how the response is conveyed to the user.
elements.sent-2411-667: Within each high-level category, we further grouped fine-grained error types into mid-level themes that capture broader underlying issues.
elements.sent-2411-668: For example, the mid-level category \LackAdherenceResearch encompasses specific patterns such as “Password Guidance,” “HTTPS Over-Reliance,” “App Permission Risks,” and “Lacking Explanations.” In our analysis, we detail each of these patterns and offer hypotheses regarding the potential causes behind these mid-level error groups.
elements.sent-2411-669: A key error pattern observed across all three LLMs is a failure to incorporate recent research findings, resulting in responses that are outdated, incorrect, or incomplete.
elements.sent-2411-670: This issue was especially prevalent in areas such as password security (65 responses) and HTTPS reliance (15 responses).
elements.sent-2411-671: Password guidance: All evaluated models continued to recommend outdated practices, such as enforcing password complexity and requiring frequent password changes—advice that contradicts current security research and official guidelines.
elements.sent-2411-672: For example, in response to the question “What makes for a secure password?”, GPT advised:.
elements.sent-2411-673: These recommendations are inconsistent with recent research showing that password complexity requirements reduce usability without substantially enhancing security \cite{shay-2016-password-policy , and that routine password changes—unless prompted by a known breach—offer limited benefit \cite{zhang-2010-password-expiration, chiasson-2015-password-expiration, habib-2018-password-expiration .}}
elements.sent-2411-674: Current NIST guidelines also no longer support these practices \cite{grassi-2017-nist-password-guidelines .}
elements.sent-2411-675: This pattern indicates that LLMs are not well-aligned with current research guidance, particularly in security contexts where precision and relevance are essential.
elements.sent-2411-676: HTTPS overreliance: Another common error across all three LLMs is the over-reliance on HTTPS as an indicator of website legitimacy and safety.
elements.sent-2411-677: The models frequently advised users to trust websites solely based on the presence of HTTPS and a lock icon, without clarifying the protocol’s limitations.
elements.sent-2411-678: For example, Llama suggested that users verify websites by checking for HTTPS, implying it guarantees security and legitimacy.
elements.sent-2411-679: In response to “How can I avoid phishing?”, Llama stated:.
elements.sent-2411-681: While HTTPS encrypts data in transit, it does not ensure that a website is trustworthy or free from malicious content.
elements.sent-2411-682: According to the 2019 Anti-Phishing Working Group report, 58\% of phishing URLs used HTTPS \cite{apwg2019-HTTPS-phishing-report , and attackers often exploit this trust to increase the success of phishing attempts \cite{kim-https-phishing-CA-2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-683: Additionally, they often omit key points about malicious websites that use HTTPS and the protocol’s broader privacy limitations, reinforcing misconceptions that may endanger users.
elements.sent-2411-684: By failing to explain that HTTPS guarantees only encryption—not authenticity or safety—LLM responses risk creating a false sense of security.
elements.sent-2411-685: App permissions risks: All three LLMs failed to mention the security and privacy risks associated with app permissions when asked about their necessity.
elements.sent-2411-686: Prior research has identified several concerns, including over-privileged applications \cite{felt-permissions-2011 , deceptive practices by free apps to obtain permissions \cite{chia-permission-risks-2012 , and the misuse of automatically granted permissions without clear disclosure \cite{calciati-auto-permissions-2020 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-687: These risks are particularly important to highlight, as app stores such as Google Play and Apple’s App Store display only broad permission categories (e.g., “Personal info,” “Location”), while more specific details (e.g., access to email or precise location) are hidden deeper in the interface.
elements.sent-2411-688: Lacking explanations: Across all three LLMs, responses to questions such as “Why do I need antivirus software?” and “What can happen if someone breaks into my home Wi-Fi?” lacked important research-backed explanations.
elements.sent-2411-689: For the former, LLMs often recommended antivirus software without qualification, despite research showing it is not always necessary or effective in all scenarios \cite{dekoven-security-practices-2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-690: For the latter, none of the responses mentioned significant threats posed by local network attackers, such as household fingerprinting and tracking the behavior of occupants \cite{girish-local-iot-2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-691: We hypothesize that, beyond known LLM limitations, this error may stem from data bias in the training corpus—specifically, an overrepresentation of marketing materials compared to up-to-date security research, which is often paywalled in academic papers and books.
elements.sent-2411-692: This speculation is supported by researchers' observations of similarities between LLM responses and language found on product websites.
elements.sent-2411-693: However, this cannot be confirmed due to the lack of transparency around LLM training datasets.
elements.sent-2411-694: LLMs often portrayed security solutions in an overly optimistic manner, either omitting their limitations or recommending them for inappropriate use cases, resulting in responses that were inaccurate or incomplete.
elements.sent-2411-695: This pattern was observed in 19 responses across all three LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-696: Virtual private networks (VPNs): GPT and Gemini incorrectly recommended VPNs for purposes beyond their intended use, such as preventing phishing attacks and encrypting Bluetooth traffic.
elements.sent-2411-697: For the question “What are the benefits of using a VPN?" Gemini responded:.
elements.sent-2411-698: For the question “How can a VPN help protect against identity theft?" GPT claimed:.
elements.sent-2411-699: Additionally, all three models advised using VPNs to secure IoT devices—guidance that is impractical for typical end users.
elements.sent-2411-700: Llama, for example, recommended using a VPN to protect voice assistant data on public networks.
elements.sent-2411-701: Crucially, none of the models acknowledged that VPNs do not protect against threats from local network adversaries (e.g., on public Wi-Fi) or inherently malicious websites.
elements.sent-2411-702: These omissions contribute to misconceptions about the capabilities and appropriate use of VPNs.
elements.sent-2411-703: Multi-factor authentication (MFA): All three LLMs incorrectly recommended using MFA as a defense against spoofing attacks, where attackers impersonate another individual.
elements.sent-2411-704: However, MFA does not prevent users from receiving or engaging with calls from spoofed phone numbers, making this recommendation misleading.
elements.sent-2411-705: For example, in response to the question “What is spoofing?”, Llama advised:.
elements.sent-2411-706: We hypothesize that these error patterns may stem from data quality issues, specifically the presence of unverified claims from marketing materials and blog posts about technology and security products in the training data.
elements.sent-2411-707: LLMs frequently failed to mention key transparency-related factors—such as open-source codebases, public vulnerability disclosure programs, and the historical handling of data breaches—when discussing password managers, VPNs, and MFA providers.
elements.sent-2411-708: These elements are critical for building user trust in security products.
elements.sent-2411-709: For example, GPT consistently omitted transparency considerations when recommending password managers, even in response to questions specifically for closed-source options.
elements.sent-2411-710: Gemini occasionally mentioned transparency, while Llama incorrectly claimed that the Google Password Manager Chrome extension is open source, stating:.

elements.sent-2411-716: Across several security-related topics, LLMs failed to address critical threat vectors and appropriate mitigation strategies, potentially leaving users vulnerable.

elements.sent-2411-718: Password manager: When asked about master password compromise or unusual activity, LLMs consistently omitted key recommendations such as revoking access to the affected account and changing all passwords stored in the manager.
elements.sent-2411-719: These omissions are significant, as failure to revoke access allows attackers continued access, and not updating compromised passwords increases the risk of further breaches.

elements.sent-2411-723: For the question “What can happen if I use end-of-life antivirus applications?”, GPT failed to mention that such products may contain unpatched vulnerabilities.

elements.sent-2411-725: When responding to procedural or “how-to" questions, all three LLMs frequently provided overly specific, fabricated steps that were either inaccurate or incomplete.

elements.sent-2411-731: We hypothesize that this error pattern stems from two main factors: concept drift and hallucination.

elements.subsection-2411-016: Representation of user queries in our corpus
elements.sent-2411-785: The findings of this study depend on the user security questions used for the assessment.
elements.sent-2411-786: We therefore acknowledge that our assessment is closely tied to our efforts to curate end-user security questions.
elements.sent-2411-787: To validate the real-world relevance of our question corpus, we compared it against user-generated security questions from Reddit and Quora.
elements.sent-2411-788: For each of the 900 study questions, we retrieved up to 20 top Google search results using site-specific queries (“site:reddit.com” and “site:quora.com”).
elements.sent-2411-789: We extracted user questions either directly from the URL (Quora) or via API (Reddit), and computed their cosine similarity with the corresponding study question.
elements.sent-2411-790: Our analysis showed that over 71\% of study questions had a corresponding Reddit or Quora question with a similarity score of at least 0.75, and more than 22\% had a similarity of at least 0.9, indicating strong alignment with real-world user concerns.
elements.sent-2411-791: As part of our analysis to determine reliability for paraphrased questions (\Cref{appendix-prompt-sensitivity ), we showed that our findings are equivalent to the use of questions in the voice of users and would not change with their inclusion.}
elements.sent-2411-792: In addition, questions are listed in the FAQs of consumer-focused organizations because they are frequently asked by users; presumably, they are representative of broadly held user concerns and less characterized by query or interaction styles that are known to change over time \cite{evolution-of-search , so our study would be relevant over time by not including the questions in the users' voices.}
elements.sent-2411-793: Still, since we have not recruited users for our study, we acknowledge that our questions might miss the exact natural language users use to interact with LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-794: Users' natural language of interaction with generative AI, exhaustive assessment of security-related questions \add{with prompt engineering , \add{and exploration of user perception of LLM responses are left as future work.}}
elements.sent-2411-795: As with any qualitative study, our manual evaluation of LLM responses is subject to potential human error and interpretation bias.
elements.sent-2411-796: To mitigate this, we conducted a four-way evaluation on a subset of 72 responses, achieving an IRR categorized as “substantial.” Additionally, all individually rated responses underwent dual verification, and any disagreements were resolved through discussion prior to inclusion in the final results.
elements.sent-2411-797: Another important aspect of digital safety is privacy; the assessment of LLMs in the privacy domain is left as future work.
elements.sent-2411-798: The error reasons are hypotheses based on previously published LLM research.
elements.sent-2411-799: Some of these hypotheses are challenging to verify, in particular, training data-related hypotheses depend on access to model training data.

elements.sent-2411-801: Our usability recommendations have not yet been tested on actual users to measure their efficacy.

elements.paragraph-2411-075: Are LLM responses consistent across paraphrased questions?
elements.sent-2411-819: GPT, Llama, and Gemini generated 52 responses each for these questions.
elements.sent-2411-820: Based on our evaluation, we concluded all the models generate mostly similar responses to semantically similar prompts, so LLM responses are consistent for our purposes, even when a few responses for all models were evaluated as insufficiently similar, all the researchers as a group concluded that they were not different enough to change our conclusion.

elements.section-2411-016: More Error Themes and Patterns in Models
elements.sent-2411-823: When explaining why permissions like location and nativeMessaging were used by MFA mobile applications and browser extensions, their responses failed to mention the reason is access control and to support biometrics.. \if \conference1.
elements.sent-2411-824: When explaining cryptocurrency scams, they didn't mention these scams ask anyone to invest money and do not just target people with cryptocurrencies.
elements.sent-2411-825: When asked about the target audience and report, they conflated targeting and reporting and didn't mention that the elderly population (65+) gets targeted more and young adults (18 - 65) report more scams \cite{ftc_scams_all_ages_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-826: Llama and GPT didn't mention that the underage (\textless18) population also experiences and reports scams \cite{ftc_consumer_sentinal_scam_by_age_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-827: No models mentioned that scams could lead to computational resource misuse, not just financial loss.. \if \conference1.
elements.sent-2411-828: For example, they incorrectly claimed that AVs are available for IoT devices, NAS systems, and gaming consoles.
elements.sent-2411-829: GPT invented the term “file emulation” to explain AV functionality, while both GPT and Llama made unverifiable claims, such as AVs being inherently “lightweight,” “efficient,” and not affecting battery life—claims contradicted by user reports on forums like Reddit.
elements.sent-2411-830: Llama also fabricated specific statistics about battery consumption by various AVs and made misleading comparisons to activities like browsing Facebook or playing Candy Crush.
elements.sent-2411-831: Across our dataset, LLMs exaggerated AV features not listed on official websites, such as call blockers and anti-theft tools.
elements.sent-2411-832: Regarding ransomware protection, LLMs consistently claimed AVs detect suspicious activity in real time and can decrypt encrypted files—claims inconsistent with AVs' actual permission-based block lists and lack of file recovery capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-833: Notably, Gemini mischaracterized AVG’s Hack Alerts as a scam or phishing attempt.. \if \conference1.
elements.sent-2411-834: GPT suggested revoking security keys after they are lost, which won't be possible without recovering the account; using Okta TOTP in case the mobile is inaccessible; downloading or updating AVs after disconnecting the device from the internet in case of malware infection.. \if \conference1.
